crime: She kept a book of accounts — everything given and taken, dated, nothing written off — and a marker-box where small things of those who owed her waited as pledges. The other girls knew about both.
A ribbon she had given the friend months before appeared at another girl's wrist. That night she drew a line under the friend's page.
During free time she took from the friend's shelf the comb the friend had kept since before the prison and laid it in the marker-box.
At dusk the friend sat at the courtyard fountain, as every evening. In her own cell, she sank the comb in a filled wash-pail and held it under.

The friend was found a step from the fountain rim, drowned. Hair and sleeves dry, no mark of any hand on the body. Girls at the second-floor railing had watched the courtyard through dusk; no one had crossed it.
The friend's comb was gone from the shelf — found damp in the marker-box beside the emptied wash-pail.
The act carried to the owner, not the water. Only she could have drowned the friend through it.
fields.epitaph: The only debt she wanted paid was staying, and no ledger could hold it.
execution: She was seated on an iron chair at the center of the execution stand, ringed by small iron hatches in the floor, each one dated.
Behind each hatch waited a charge of coals; beneath the chair, a channel.
A slow clock opened the first hatch on the stroke — its coals ran down the channel. The next opened at the next stroke, and the next, each feeding its charge beneath her.
When every hatch stood open and the channel held its full measure, she burned dead in the chair.

magic.name: Marker Due
magic.text: What she does to a thing another has long kept as their own, held now in her keeping, is done in kind to its owner.
